speaker: interviewer
text: Stepak, 11/6/96.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they was going with a stick, and saying, [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] mean cow, cows. It was cows with milk. And they come in the first room, [NON-ENGLISH]. In the second room. It's like, first room, the second room, and this is the third room. [NON-ENGLISH]. And I see it come to the room. We's laying there with my wife, under the sheep. And he start to open the door. That time, God helped me. I was-- when he opened up the door, I was talking to God, it's true. You helped me up to now. Help me-- Help me [NON-ENGLISH]. And he helped me. He closed the door. He went back. We-- we survived. I know that. That is-- that is true. If you talk to God-- I can never talk now to God real. When you talk to God real, you've got to help me. That's what's mean. And he helped me. And he closed the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he either went-- when he went in there and put the stick, he got us. That's it. See? He let us go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's inbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we see it's not a place. And the Polish people see that too. Then we went all together. The Polish there know where to go. Then we went in the side from the Polish people. Over there, they was free already. There was-- there was Germans there, too, but they don't-- they don't bother the Polish people. And we went with the Polish people together. When we went with the Polish people there, I was there with my wife and one place I know them too, from [NON-ENGLISH] near [NON-ENGLISH], went there. They feed us a good couple days. And we stay with him and then we went farther. The SS-- the SS after the [NON-ENGLISH], they are partisan, [NON-ENGLISH], in German, too. Over there, you can go free. That's where we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what year this was, first of all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was like, maybe, second of '42, maybe. They started up '42, I believe. They can't be '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: They can't be. They must have been '42 already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now this is in '42. And you went with the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went with the Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the Polish people and the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I didn't call them-- I call--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You were with the Polish people. Did you hide your Judaism?

words.text: OK. You were with the Polish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: They know I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said-- they knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They know Jewish-- but not everybody. Not everybody, because we-- when we was walking, the Polish people, they was doing-- the Ukrainian people was doing to the Polish people they must leave. They come in. They find the Polish people. They take out from the stomach everything, and turn around the tree. They take away the cows and everything. That's what they were doing before to the Jewish people. There was no Jewish, they started working with the Polish people. Then we went with them. We was going-- we was going Polish people, like I tell you, from here like to Glasbury. So many people--

speaker: interviewer
text: 30 miles.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So many long people was-- so many people. And the German was see there. The German see the [INAUDIBLE]. They don't [INAUDIBLE]. They see the German, Ukrainian, [? run ?] with the with the Polish people. We was in between maybe a dozen Jewish people between that group. Nobody ever mind. Nobody knows us. We was a couple. They know us. We stick to them together. They was good to us. And we went. Then we went there, then everybody split. And we find out more-- more Jewish survivors. They have a couple more there. Then we went-- they we went together there, tried to do something, to work. I was working with the-- with the Ukrainians. I was helping with the hay, and I was helping-- a lot of things. I-- I know how to do it. They was like me. They give me just to eat [INAUDIBLE]. And sleep, I was sleeping in-- in the [? barn ?] over there, in the-- me and my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What I'm confused with is that why nobody turned you over to the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no time, that time. They was afraid. They have their problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Poles were afraid of the Ukrainians and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They they have their problems. They didn't have time to to turn over to the to Polish. The all Jews was killed. There was no Jewish that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so Germans didn't think to look for Jews because they thought they killed all the Jews already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that is Polish people just walking. And we went over there. Then we went with the partisans. And they took me in the partisans, me and my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- It was there. What was there? I couldn't tell. There was the other side-- the other site, the other side Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was mostly their different-- what do you call there, the place? I couldn't remember. I couldn't remember. But there was all partisan there. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whole-- the whole woods, all over partisan. And the people was together with the partisan, the partisan with the people there. And we went there. And we was in-- having Otriad. Otriad [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a Otriad [NON-ENGLISH] was the-- the main-- the main-- the main [INAUDIBLE], [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't understand what you mean by 'tra.' That's okay.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Otriad mean the whole-- the whole group--

speaker: interviewer
text: Troop? The whole group?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole group partisan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name from the main head was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That was the name of the-- that was the name of the partisan group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the head of the group? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The person-- oh, that was the name of the-- name of the leader.

speaker: interviewee
text: The leader [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In that-- and that name, the whole thing was calling [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. And how many Jews were together with you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was not many Jews in that-- in that group.

speaker: interviewer
text: About a dozen? Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not more, a dozen. But he was treating us good, [NON-ENGLISH]. He was treating me good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you live? How did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the partisan have plenty food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay? Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, with the partisan. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- did you have a camp, a bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Camps. Camps. Camps, bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tents or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tents. He was building up himself. Everything the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you engage any battles, fighting? Fighting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a gun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They gave me a gun. I was fighting with them. We was putting in under trains, was ripping up the trains. In the most, they put me in-- I was a [NON-ENGLISH], doing-- doing skin, like sheeps sometime I'm making-- I'm making from that to wear, from sheep, the skin. I was a [NON-ENGLISH]. They call [NON-ENGLISH]. But its in Yiddish, called-- they call [NON-ENGLISH] in Jewish. I don't know. And  they mostly put me in for [NON-ENGLISH]. I was working. My wife was working there too, help in the kitchen what we need. And we was happy. We was happy there. Soon-- soon they come. And even that time, I got a baby, too. All of the sudden, I got a baby, six months old. The name was Szise, after my father. It was a nice little boy. Even the [NON-ENGLISH] used to come play with him. And they give me even a cow with milk for the baby. For the-- they was calling him the boss from the Otriad. What happened the end, when when the German, when they start running the German back, and they was coming in our neighborhood. And they coming in. Let's say like [NON-ENGLISH] was free already from the German. Now [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], it was very hard to go straight. Then we have to go all around. In-- in that time, my wife was sick on typhus, on typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Typhus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhus, yes. And she didn't have how to feed the baby. And we couldn't get milk. And the baby pass away that time. Then we come without the baby. [INAUDIBLE]. Pass away the baby. I think everything happen strange, strange happen. Then we come back to [NON-ENGLISH]. When we come back to [NON-ENGLISH], then the same. We have Holocaust live in Zdolbunov, near Rovno. There was a lot of Holocaust people left over from our people. Then we tried to go to-- to Zdolbunov, to Rovno, to Rovno there. Finally, we came. We find a lot of relative-- not relative, just friend the leftover. And a lot of was not gone even in-- in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this-- where were the Germans at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans was running back already. They was already on the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is like, it's already 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: End of 1943 and beginning of 1944. Something the end. They was run already. They was chasing them back that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me some more-- because it seemed like we-- we went real fast through your whole episode with the partisans. You said you were sabotaging the railroad tracks. You were lifting up the railroad tracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you actually in any battles? Were you fighting the Ukrainian? Were  you fighting the Germans, or both?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans. Only Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're fighting the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Germans. We was not fighting. We couldn't fight them. We only putted in mines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mines.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mines. We only put it in mines. That's one thing. Was a lot of killed, too. Was a lot of killed, too. I was hurt too, here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shot, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, yeah. What helped me, I-- I [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] all right, thank God. Is-- we was doing. And we was happy to do it. I was happy to do it. Was-- I was not scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you never were in a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that concentration camp Kostopol.

speaker: interviewer
text: The labor--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear what was going on in the death camps? Did you know about Auschwitz and--

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know nothing about Auschwitz. What I know about Auschwitz when I see, in the television, what they run the-- the bulldozers to people, it's just inbelievable. Inbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that Hitler, [NON-ENGLISH]. What is-- he's a man, like any man. What does that mean? I don't want to talk about. Couldn't God made him paralyzed or something? What is this? You take a man? You take live people and he killed them. Live people and he killed them. And the whole world was saying nothing. Tell me, when America wants to do something, or England, they couldn't do it? They couldn't-- they couldn't do maybe right away over there. They couldn't help-- they could help a lot. So you-- but when you were on-- you never heard about that the people were being in the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard about it after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the way they were killing people where you lived was they were shooting them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shooting. Right. We-- we have our own problems there. We-- we don't have time to think about something.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see the problems right behind our eyes. Anybody, even a little boy, walks, you go [INAUDIBLE]. You don't want him to see it. Everybody was-- everybody was scared, surviving.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why-- why is the surviving so strong? Why is so strong everybody wants survive? You go without shoes. You go in the snow. You don't have nothing to eat. You hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you still want to survive. Life is-- life is very important, must be. Very important, life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So ultimately, at-- you-- you-- when the Germans ran away, you-- you went away from the partisans. And you found people back from Rovno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Survivors, Rovno they come in. Even from the [NON-ENGLISH] I find a couple people, from the [NON-ENGLISH]. They now in Israel. We meet each other when I come there. When I come now to Israel, always make [NON-ENGLISH], take old [NON-ENGLISH], all the survivors, [NON-ENGLISH] together. And I make them a dinner, and we talk. We talk. We talk. We talk and never finish talking what we went through. We never finish talking  what we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next? When you went back-- I forget the name of the town that you said where you found everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is when we went back, you mean, to Rovno, the survivors?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you went back to Rovno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is, like, 1944 already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. After the-- after the-- after the-- after the war? It was after the war, already.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- did you encounter the-- did the Russians come in there yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were the Russians there. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were Russians there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what happened next for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened next for me, then when we stayed here and we-- in Zdolbunov. And then they says-- they says they was making us-- like what they making us like Greeks. There was organization, Jewish organization to help us, to get us farther. Then we can go to Israel, like to Israel to move us. Then they make us for Greeks. And they transport us, everybody, to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a time that you were in the Russian army? Am I mistaken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I been in the Russian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did we skip over that, or is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I skipped that. No. We don't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back and talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Talk about the Russian army. What did the Russian army, they take us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When? When?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take us when we come to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: This is-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] after the-- after the-- everything with the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they-- then took-- then the Russians took the whole-- there was-- we was about 30-- about 30 youngsters, about 30 men, maybe. And they took us back on the front. And they took us on the front. And they took us to [NON-ENGLISH] to learn us how to shoot, how to-- how to do it when we come on the war. Then we was four weeks in [INAUDIBLE], they call this, what they teach us, four weeks. And after that, they took us on the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: What front?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been-- went through Latvia, Estonia, we went through. And there were some days-- some days was the-- the-- the-- the German was so running and we couldn't run after that. Some-- some days, they was running so fast back. We couldn't run. We was-- we was saying to ourself, ah, we just stop a minute. We don't fill the food, nothing, because you have to walk after that. Have to walk. And then-- then we went on the-- then we went again on the front. And in the front was-- he send us-- he send us-- the commandeer, the head of the Otriad, the head. He sends us to go see what's going on. We were staying this side was a lake. And the other side was the German. He always keep me near him. He always keep me. When something, you go, he take me with me him. He trust me. Now this time, he tell me to go-- to go and see what going on there, over the lake. Three men, it took us. But I was not-- I was never run the first one, and never the last one. Always go, like, in the middle, see what happen. And they start shooting on us. They start shooting on us. When they are shooting on us, they shoot one. One run away, and they catch me. That's where they catch me here. That's where they catch me here. And finally, they took me back. That was my luck. Like those things, they going to cut up the end, they cutted up the end, the legs, everybody. Over there, they cut me out the end. And they took me back to [NON-ENGLISH]-- that's a burg back in Russia. And they took me there. They keep me there six months. I was lucky. Six months they keep me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: In hospital. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your wife at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife was in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did she know-- she just knew you were in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She knows I'm in army. I was writing letters to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I was telling her even I'm hurt, but not bad. And she was thinking I'm bad hurt. And then we went there. And finally, I got a Jewish-- there was Jewish doctor there. And she give me [NON-ENGLISH], come on, go back to [NON-ENGLISH]. She gave me six months off to go three months and three months. And when we come there, the-- I was-- I was trying not to get healed. I don't want to go back to the army. But anyway, they was getting healed. In the meantime, the war was finished. That's why--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were trying so it doesn't get healed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I'm afraid to go again on the front.

words.text: to go again on the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. How were you trying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was trying, to truth tell you, I putted in a penny, like a penny, copper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They was holding up a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Make it a little infected. Keep it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A little. And they keep me another three months. And then she sent me to [NON-ENGLISH] home. And finally, the war was finished. And that was the end. That was the end. Then we went, like I told you, with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish organized--

speaker: interviewee
text: Organized, like, what they call us?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not the Haganah. The--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was-- that was not Haganah. It was-- they was-- was working, very hard working with that, very good working with that, too. A lot of people was gone. Like Greeks-- Greeks, they sent us like Greeks. The Greeks was that time something the other side, you know. The Greeks clothes with the berets, and Greeks. When the Russian come talk to us, we couldn't talk to them back Russian. We talk to them like we pray Jewish. They come to me, and they talk, I say [NON-ENGLISH]. And they saying-- they saying-- I hear what they are saying because we know it. They saying, [NON-ENGLISH]. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw dummies. Such dummies like the Greeks, he says, I never saw in my life. See, they talked to us. And we asked them-- we answered them like they told us to do that, not to-- not to say a word Russian, because they can hold us up right away. And they say, we saw dummies, such dummies like the Greeks, you don't see it. I was praying to them. They talking to me, and I pray to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like [NON-ENGLISH]. And they looking at me. And they hollering me. And I pray again to them. Then they say, [NON-ENGLISH]. It was fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they left you alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They left us alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went to Germany. We went to [NON-ENGLISH]. Then we went from [NON-ENGLISH] to Austria-- Austria, I believe. We been in Austria. They give us rooms there, and food, everything. We was waiting to call us apply to America, to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in American camp in-- in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: American DP camp? Do you remember the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No. Just they give us hotels. They give us hotels.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] stay in hotels. Everybody was a lot of, a lot of Jewish refugees there.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. How long were you in Germany for?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, we was there a good year, maybe. I come here 1949. Then, from that time, I was in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Germany for a few years, for-- for--

speaker: interviewee
text: A good--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Augustine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Augustine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in Germany? Did you work there? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they give us work. The camp, alone, give us work. The Jewish camp, we was having work. We have to go to cook, and sometimes clean up. We didn't get pay. We didn't get pay because we get free-- free food there. There was-- from America, they supply that. And finally, they called me to America. Me and my wife, we come to America. We come. We got here, find our uncle, our tante.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop. You're going too fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you get to America, ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was a ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mercy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, I remember. ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that-- what was the ship like? A lot of Jews on the ship? How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was mixed. Not Jewish alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mixed. We have to paint the the ship. We're painting. They give us painting, because they give us food, we have to paint. We paint. I learn how to paint. And we come in the ship to America. And America, then we're in America. We didn't see America. America, my goodness. We couldn't open our eyes, the-- the lights, and the-- and the cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we come here. Mine uncle's son pick us up with a car. We were thinking, what's going on here? We never saw that. And finally, we looking for jobs, like I was-- Where did you come in America? To New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brooklyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Brooklyn, we come. And we stayed there with the-- with the uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tante [INAUDIBLE], he pass away. And Tante pass away, too, but the kids is still alive, [NON-ENGLISH]. He's my wife's-- my wife's uncle. My wife's.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, we've been by him. And when-- and I find me a job. I looking for a job. I couldn't talk English. It was not easy. But the people was very good to me. Anybody-- I have a list, like I want to go on that-- that place. I catch a man. I show him. And he took me there. I can't talk English. I can't talk-- he took me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was look any work, anything I see. I was caps, [POINTS] to do caps. So I figure I'll go do caps. I was young, strong. So I do caps. And then I come to the caps. He says, I want him to-- the boss says, if you know how to ride a machine, I'll take you. But I don't know how to start a machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I say to the man there, a Jewish man, I say, can you show me how to start the machine? He says, come over Saturday. I say, why Saturday? Because Saturday, I say, I wouldn't be here. [LAUGHS] That's a funny--

speaker: interviewee
text: First he is telling me to go buy a cap. I was going without a cap. We buy a cap, because we'll be making caps, have to show. I said, then I looked for another job. I find a job later-- I find a job later for $0.72 sell pickles. I was selling pickles at a Jewish place. I make $20 that week. It was very good. I have a full fridge there. There was plenty time-- plenty problem before I get a room, before I get what-- it was very hard to get a-- a place to live. Then I find a place that was like a grocery, a front-- a front room and a little room on the side, in the back, a little kitchen. And I was lucky I get that room. I put me in a little Frigidaire. And I was looking for a job. I find a job right on Watkins Street, right there next door with that man with the pickles. They want to kill me. They-- they-- they-- they-- I worked with black people. And I would have to carry barrels to give him. And he took a barrel, he jumped on me. That guy, the black man, and I was-- get very mad. And I jump out from that hole. And I took him. I want to kill him. I want to kill him. I was choking him. He was black [INAUDIBLE]. And everybody comes to me to hold me up. And I explain him why. He took a barrel, want to kill me. Then after that, nobody was touching me. They was talking, don't you  touch that refugee, that one. After that, I was looking for another job. I find-- I find a job, a butcher. And a butcher couldn't make a living, then three days a week. Then I find [INAUDIBLE] veal market. I was making out good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What market?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] veal market, they call it. Calfs. They're boiling calfs. And I was making $190 a week there. It was a very good job, very-- I was working hard. I not afraid from working hard. And I was making some time, with overtime-- I don't work Saturdays, Sundays, sometime. I was making the 20 and the 30. You wouldn't believe that. In a short time, I save up about $4,500, $5,000 that time. It's inbelievable. Then everybody say, go buy a farm, a chicken farm. They're doing good. And I went to Newfield here in [INAUDIBLE], and bought a chicken farm in Newfield. Then the chicken farm was good the first year. The first year was very good. And after that, went down, and I couldn't make a living with the chicken farm. Then I was looking again for a job. I went to work for [NON-ENGLISH]. He then-- then he couldn't hold me. Then I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], he was around here. He was living in [INAUDIBLE]. You should be having that name. [INAUDIBLE], Mr. Gold, you know? Mr. Gold was there with chicken farm, Gold. And that's [INAUDIBLE}-- that's [NON-ENGLISH] brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work were you doing for them? Also a chicken farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Farmers market. Farmers market. He was going in the farmers market and I was working for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your chicken farm didn't do good, what did you-- just close it down, sold it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, then I work with him to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: To survive. Everything is to survive. And then-- I'll make it short. And then, after that, he fire me. He lay me off. And then I have nothing to do. Then I went to my friend in Colk-- that [INAUDIBLE], and ask him what to do. I have to do something I can make a living. I have two kids that time. And he says, get in this same business. I'll borrow you money. I'll help you. Then I went and start looking for a truck. With a truck, I was very-- too much guts to do it. The guts was too much. I was not afraid for nothing. I don't have the money. I bought a truck. The bank give me a mortgage on the truck, Newfield Bank. It's inbelievable. When I tell you, another day is not enough to tell the story, [INAUDIBLE] year even. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- you took the truck, and you were selling eggs on the market?

speaker: interviewee
text: No eggs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you selling on the market?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men's clothing, basket, eggs, everything mixed up, and I start making money. When I finish the year, I make $7,000. When I saw $7,000, I'll prove next year I'll make double and double and double. And I came out very good. Then, after that, I went to Dover, Delaware. And I stayed 20 years in Dover, Delaware. I make out very good. Then-- then I bought-- then I went in the other business, the motel business. And I left Dover, Delaware. Now I'm in the motel business. From the whole thing, I'll be glad now to get out from the motel business too. That's enough. Work, work, work, work. 20 years in the [INAUDIBLE], 28 years in the motel business. And I'm getting older. I'm not-- I'm not getting younger. But one man can get through. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK. Is-- the end is, I'm happy. I'm glad, everything. I pass-- My first wife passed away. Then I married my second wife in the same time. I'm very happy with her too. I have nice three kids. They always care about me. They're calling me like every week. They are calling me. And Michael, the-- the smallest, it was a accident. It was a accident he was born. My first wife wants to get rid of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we went, I remember, to a doctor. And the doctor says, why you want to get rid of him? Have me the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Give it to me. I'll take the baby. There was a [NON-ENGLISH] man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we come to-- to farmers market. And there's a lady say, why do you want to get rid of him? Give me the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then she say, he want the baby. They want the baby. Then she changed her mind and we have the baby. Thanks God that. He's a very good, nice man, Mike. Anything, anything is right away with me. Anything-- anything right away. And--

words.text: Then she say, he want the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of the other kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is Eileen. She's in-- she's in Brooklyn, lives there. Very nice girl. And I got the elder son, Asar. He's in-- he's in temple, makes a living. And I got Mike. He went for a lawyer, chief lawyer. Now he's going for a doctor. You can't tell him what to do.
